content.value: Unlike arrays, linked lists store elements in separate nodes connected by pointers, scattered anywhere in memory. This makes insertion/deletion O(1) once you have a pointer to the right spot, at the cost of O(n) access time (no direct indexing) and extra memory for pointers.

Each node has two pointers: next and prev. This allows traversal in both directions and makes deletion of a known node O(1) without needing to track a 'previous' pointer separately, at the cost of extra memory per node.

The last node's next pointer points back to the first node instead of NULL, forming a ring. Useful for round-robin scheduling or any application that needs to cycle through elements repeatedly. Traversal must check 'have I returned to the head?' instead of checking for NULL.
Reversing a singly linked list in-place requires re-pointing each node's next pointer to the previous node, walking through with three pointers: prev, cur, and next.
In list-reversal exam questions, draw the three pointers (prev, cur, next) on paper at each step - it's the most common place students lose track of which pointer moves when.

quiz.question: What is the main advantage of a linked list over an array?
options: O(1) insertion/deletion once you have the right pointer, no shifting needed
Linked lists use less memory per element

quiz.question: In a circular linked list, the last node's next pointer points to:
Reversing a singly linked list in place typically uses how many tracking pointers?
